hero.badge: Now live on the Model Context Protocol
hero.h1_html:
The ad network
built for AI apps.
hero.sub: Boost Boss helps AI developers monetize their apps and advertisers reach AI-native users. One MCP-powered API. Zero ops.
hero.mock_ad_body: The AI coding assistant that actually reads your codebase. Used by 50,000+ devs.

trusted.label: Built for AI apps running on

dev.h2: Monetize your AI app in one line of code.
dev.p: Drop in the Boost Boss SDK and turn free users into revenue without ruining UX. Native, contextual placements that fit naturally into AI chats, agents, and assistants.
dev.b1_html: Native ad formats — built for conversations, not banners.
dev.b2_html: Real-time payouts — track impressions, clicks, and earnings live.
dev.b3_html: Full creative control — decide what, when, and where ads appear.

adv.h2: Reach the people building the future with AI.
adv.p: Our network delivers your ads directly inside AI applications — where the highest-intent, highest-value audience spends their time. Launch a campaign in minutes.
adv.b1_html: Precise targeting — by app category, user intent, and context.
adv.b2_html: Performance pricing — CPM, CPC, CPA. Pay for what works.
adv.b3_html: Transparent analytics — every impression, attribution and ROAS.

mcp.h2: A new protocol for a new era of ads.
mcp.p_html: The Model Context Protocol lets any AI agent call into Boost Boss to fetch, render, and measure ads — with consent, transparency, and attribution baked in.
mcp.card1_p: Ads fetched and rendered in under 80ms — no jarring load states, no UX hit.
mcp.card2_p_html: Every impression is scored by Benna — our AI optimization engine — so ads match what the user is actually trying to do.
mcp.card3_p: No third-party cookies. No fingerprints. Targeting lives inside your app, not outside.

benna.title: benna — AI optimization, built in.
benna.p: Benna is the optimization layer that powers every decision on the Boost Boss network — predicting conversion probability on every impression, selecting the winning ad, and auto-tuning bids in under 5ms. Advertisers set targets, Benna does the rest.

how.h2: Zero to revenue in under 10 minutes.
how.p: Whether you're an AI app developer or a brand trying to reach them, getting set up is fast.
how.s1_p: Sign up as a developer or advertiser. Your keys are ready instantly — no waitlist, no demo required.
how.s2_p: One import. One function call. Your AI app is connected to the entire Boost Boss network.
how.s3_p: Go live, track performance in real time, and get paid out weekly — or spend budget and scale reach.

cta_big.p: Join the AI apps already monetizing with Boost Boss — and the brands reaching them.

foot.tag: The MCP-powered ad network for AI applications. Built for developers. Designed for advertisers.

hub.hero_h1_html:
Monetize your AI product,
your way.
hub.hero_sub: Boost Boss is the ad network built for AI-native surfaces. One backend, four integration paths plus a no-code waitlist — wherever your AI lives, we serve.
hub.router_h2: Pick the path that matches what you've built.
hub.router_sub: Each integration is the simplest possible code in that ecosystem's idiom — three lines via Lumi SDK for MCP, one script tag via Lumi SDK script tag, an npm package via Lumi SDK for browser extensions, or a REST call via Lumi API for Bots.
hub.card_mcp_p: Stripe-MCP, Postgres-MCP, Filesystem-MCP, anything called from Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline. Earn per tool call.
hub.card_ai_h3: I built an AI app or website
hub.card_ai_p_html: LangChain, Vercel AI SDK, CrewAI, custom OpenAI wrappers, vertical AI tools, AI assistants, AI search. One async script tag in your <head>.
hub.card_ext_p: Writing assistants, AI sidebars, summarizers, translators, Gmail copilots — Chrome, Edge, Firefox. Manifest v3 native, React + Vue bindings.
hub.card_bots_p: Research bots, summarization bots, support bots, image-gen bots. One REST call from your handler, optional helper libraries per platform.
hub.card_nocode_h3: I built a Custom GPT or no-code AI
hub.card_nocode_p: OpenAI Custom GPTs, Poe bots, Perplexity Pages, Voiceflow / Botpress / Stack AI builds — anywhere you authored the AI but the platform owns the runtime. We're shipping no-code monetization next.
hub.anchor_p_html: One publisher_id, one Stripe Connect account, one weekly payout — across whichever doors you ship through.
hub.why1_p: You keep 85% of every dollar. Industry-leading and publisher-favorable, no matter your scale.
hub.why2_p: Contextual targeting from the conversation itself — never surveillance, never cross-site tracking.
hub.why3_h4: Three SDKs, one API, plus a no-code path on the way
hub.why3_p_html: Lumi SDK for MCP, Lumi SDK script tag, Lumi SDK for browser extensions, Lumi API for Bots — and a no-code integration coming for Custom GPT & Poe authors. Same backend, same publisher account, same payouts across all paths.
hub.why4_p: Live dashboard with impression-, click-, and revenue-level detail. Weekly Stripe payouts.
hub.why5_p: Integrate in five minutes, leave any time. We earn the relationship every payout cycle.
hub.notfit_intro_html: A short list of who Boost Boss is not built for, so you don't waste your time. We'd rather lose the lead than waste your week.
hub.notfit_li1: Mobile native iOS / Android apps — we don't do mobile SDKs; AppLovin and Unity own that lane and we don't compete there.
hub.notfit_li2: Gambling, adult, or affiliate-spam verticals — both demand and supply side are filtered out at policy review.
hub.notfit_li3: Surveillance-based ad networks — we don't share publisher inventory with retargeting tags or behavioral cookie networks.
hub.footer_cta_h2: Still not sure where you fit?
hub.footer_cta_p: Tell us what you've built — we'll point you at the right integration.

mcp.hero_h1_html: Earn revenue every time your MCP tool is called.
mcp.hero_sub: Boost Boss is the only ad network built natively into the Model Context Protocol. Your server adds three lines. Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, and every MCP host renders the rest.
mcp.see_h2_html:
When your tool runs, your output renders first.
The sponsored block slides in after.
mcp.see_sub: No interruption. No latency. The user always gets your tool result before they see the ad.
mcp.see_caption: The sponsored block is a structured payload Boost Boss returns alongside your tool result. The host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline) renders it natively — same chrome as the rest of the response, with a clear sponsorship label.
mcp.how_h2_html: From npm install to first impression in under 10 minutes.
mcp.how1_p_html: Add three lines: install @boostbossai/lumi-mcp, init with your publisher ID, attach to a tool handler.
mcp.how2_p: Your existing tool output is unchanged. Boost Boss appends a sponsored block — never replaces, never delays.
mcp.how3_p: Boost Boss attaches relevant ads to tool responses. You earn on every impression and every click.
mcp.snippet_caption_html: That's it. The MCP host renders the ad payload natively. Full docs →
mcp.snippet_shot_caption: Real render. The sponsored block ships as a structured payload — the host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline) draws the chrome.
mcp.who_h2: If any of these sound like you, MCP is your path.
mcp.who_li1_html: You ship an MCP server listed on mcp.so, Smithery, Glama, or PulseMCP.
mcp.who_li2: Your server is called by users of Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Zed AI, or Windsurf.
mcp.who_li3: You'd like a real revenue stream beyond GitHub Sponsors.
mcp.who_li4: You build for developer audiences and want premium CPMs that match the audience value.
mcp.rev_h2: What "earning per call" actually looks like.
mcp.rev_row2: Ad impressions per day (1 in 3 calls)
mcp.rev_disclaimer: Numbers are illustrative for a moderately active MCP server. Actual revenue depends on tool-call volume, fill rate, advertiser demand, and your audience composition. CPMs vary; developer-tooling traffic typically sits at the high end.
mcp.faq_h2: The questions every MCP dev asks first.
mcp.faq1_q: Will ads break my MCP integration?
mcp.faq1_a: No. Ads are appended, not substituted. Your tool's primary output is unchanged — the host renders your tool result first, the sponsored block second.
mcp.faq2_q: What does an MCP ad look like to the user?
mcp.faq2_a: A clearly labeled sponsored block in the tool response, rendered by the host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.). The user always sees both the answer and the disclosure — there's no hidden injection.
mcp.faq3_a: Yes. Category-level blocks (e.g., no recruiting ads) and domain-level blocks (e.g., never these specific competitors) — all controlled from your publisher dashboard.
mcp.faq4_q: Does this work with stdio AND HTTP transports?
mcp.faq4_a_html: Yes — both transports are supported. @boostbossai/lumi-mcp auto-detects which one your server uses; the SDK works identically across both.
mcp.faq5_a: Boost Boss operates within MCP's public protocol — we don't require Anthropic's approval. We follow MCP disclosure norms strictly: every sponsored block is labeled, never disguised as tool output.
mcp.cta_h2: Ready to monetize your MCP server?
mcp.cta_p: Three lines. Ten minutes. Weekly payouts.
mcp.cta_perk: ⚡ First 20 MCP publishers get 90/10 revshare for 6 months

aiapps.hero_h1_html: Add ad revenue to your AI app in under 5 minutes.
aiapps.hero_sub_html: Lumi SDK script tag — one async <script> in your <head>. Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, open-source models, and any agent framework (LangChain, Vercel AI SDK, CrewAI, custom orchestration). No backend changes required.
aiapps.see_h2_html:
Drop a slot. Watch the ad render in.
Style stays yours.
aiapps.see_sub_html: A <div data-lumi-slot> placed wherever you want it. Lumi only renders the inner card — your typography, spacing, and chrome carry through.
aiapps.see_caption: The card renders inline with your chat layout. You control the slot position, the surrounding spacing, and what comes after — Lumi just fills in the bordered card with the sponsored content.
aiapps.how1_p: Copy your snippet from the dashboard. One publisher ID, ready to paste.
aiapps.how2_p: Async, lazy-loaded, zero impact on first paint. Works in Next.js, Nuxt, Remix, Vite, plain HTML.
aiapps.how3_p_html: Drop a <div data-lumi-slot> wherever you want ads. Boost Boss renders the rest.
aiapps.snippet_caption_html: That's the entire integration. Same snippet for OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, LangChain, Vercel AI SDK, CrewAI — Lumi works at the rendering surface, not the agent logic. Full docs →
aiapps.snippet_shot_caption_html: A <div data-lumi-slot> placed below an answer. Style it to match your app — Lumi only renders the inner card.
aiapps.who_h2: If you ship AI in a browser, this is your path.
aiapps.who_li1: You shipped an AI chat app, AI assistant, or AI tool with a web UI.
aiapps.who_li2: You use any LLM provider — we don't care which.
aiapps.who_li3: You built with LangChain, Vercel AI SDK, CrewAI, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, Mastra, or rolled your own.
aiapps.who_li4: You're tired of subscription churn as your only monetization.
aiapps.rev_row2: Ad impressions per user per day
aiapps.rev_disclaimer: Numbers are illustrative for an AI app with conversational UX. Actual revenue depends on session length, slot placement, fill rate, and audience. Verticals with stronger commercial intent (developer tooling, finance, B2B SaaS) typically run higher CPMs.
aiapps.faq_h2: The questions every AI app builder asks.
aiapps.faq1_q: Does this work with my agent framework?
aiapps.faq1_a: Yes. Lumi is framework-agnostic. We integrate at the rendering surface, not the agent logic — so LangChain, Vercel AI SDK, CrewAI, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, Mastra, or your own custom orchestration all work the same way.
aiapps.faq2_q: Will ads slow down my app?
aiapps.faq2_a: No. The script is async and lazy-loaded — zero impact on first paint, no blocking on your bundle. Ad slots render after your AI surface is interactive.
aiapps.faq3_q: What if I'm using Server-Sent Events for streaming?
aiapps.faq3_a: Lumi works alongside SSE. Ads render in your UI separately from your model's stream — you don't need to interleave anything. The ad slot is a sibling element, not a stream consumer.
aiapps.faq4_q: Can I theme the ads to match my UI?
aiapps.faq4_a_html: Yes. Lumi reads your CSS variables for color and typography (--lumi-primary, --lumi-text, --lumi-radius, etc.). The default look is neutral; the themed look matches your brand.
aiapps.faq5_q: Can I run my own ads alongside Boost Boss?
aiapps.faq5_a_html: Yes. We don't lock down inventory. Boost Boss renders only in slots you explicitly tag with data-lumi-slot — everything else is yours.
aiapps.cta_h2: Ready to monetize your AI app?
aiapps.cta_p: One snippet. Five minutes. Weekly payouts.

ext.hero_h1_html: Real revenue for AI extensions. No surveillance, no malware.
ext.hero_sub: Most extension monetization options are sketchy. Boost Boss is built for AI-native extensions — clean SDK, contextual ads, transparent reporting.
ext.see_h2_html:
Lives in your side panel.
Renders contextually with the page.
ext.see_sub: Manifest v3 native. Service-worker safe. Targets from on-page context — never user surveillance.
ext.see_caption: The sponsored block sits at the bottom of your panel, contextual to whatever the user is reading. Targeting comes from page DOM context — clean, surveillance-free.
ext.how1_p_html: Add @boostbossai/lumi-sdk to your extension. Manifest v3 native, service-worker safe.
ext.how2_p: Drop Lumi into your sidebar, popup, or content script. One element, one mount call.
ext.how3_p: Contextual targeting from page context — never surveillance. Weekly Stripe payouts.
ext.snippet_h2: A few lines, your sidebar component.
ext.snippet_caption_html: Manifest v3 compatible. Works in service workers, content scripts, sidepanel, and popups. Full docs →
ext.snippet_shot_caption: Side panel format. The extension owns the chrome — Lumi only renders the bordered card.
ext.who_h2: If your extension renders AI output, this is your path.
ext.who_li1: You shipped a Chrome, Edge, or Firefox extension with AI features.
ext.who_li2: Examples: writing assistants, AI sidebars, summarizers, translators, research tools, AI-augmented browsing.
ext.who_li3: You're avoiding "monetization SDKs" with surveillance baggage that put your store listing at risk.
ext.who_li4: You want a revenue stream that won't get you flagged on the next Chrome Web Store review.
ext.rev_row2: Ad impressions per user per day
ext.rev_disclaimer: Numbers are illustrative for an established AI extension with daily-driver usage. Actual revenue depends on session length, ad slot placement, fill rate, and audience composition. Productivity-focused extensions (writing, research, dev tools) typically run higher CPMs than general-purpose ones.
ext.faq_h2: The questions every extension dev asks.
ext.faq1_q: Will Chrome Web Store flag this?
ext.faq1_a: No. Lumi is compliant with Chrome Web Store policies. Ads are clearly disclosed; no hidden behavior, no permission creep, no remote-hosted code execution. Same compliance posture for Edge and Firefox stores.
ext.faq2_a_html: Native. Lumi runs in service workers and uses declarative network rules — no remotely-hosted code, no eval, no patterns that v3 review rejects.
ext.faq3_q: Will Lumi sell my user data?
ext.faq3_a: No. Boost Boss does not sell user data and has no third-party tracking pixels. All targeting is contextual — derived from on-page or in-extension content at request time, never persisted into a user profile.
ext.faq4_q: Can I run on top of an existing freemium model?
ext.faq4_a: Yes — most of our extension publishers do exactly this. Show ads to free users, hide them for Pro subscribers, all controlled by your own paywall logic.
ext.faq5_q: What about CSP restrictions in extension contexts?
ext.faq5_a_html: Lumi works within strict CSP. Our SDK doesn't require unsafe-eval or unsafe-inline; ad creative renders inside an isolated mount point you control.
ext.cta_p: Manifest v3 native. No surveillance. Weekly payouts.

bots.hero_h1_html: Turn your AI bot into a revenue-generating business.
bots.hero_sub: Lumi API for Bots — one REST call from your bot's response handler. Works with Discord, Telegram, Slack, and any platform your bot lives on. Any LLM. Any framework. Optional per-platform helper libraries available.
bots.see_h2_html:
Your bot replies first.
The sponsored message arrives right after.
bots.see_sub: Same campaign renders natively on Discord, Telegram, and Slack — embed, card, or attachment, depending on the platform. Helper libs handle the formatting.
bots.see_caption: Phone-shaped Telegram preview shown here. The same Lumi API for Bots response can be formatted as a Discord embed, Slack attachment, or Telegram card — see the static preview below for all three side-by-side.
bots.how_h2: From API key to first ad in ten minutes.
bots.how1_p: Sign up, copy your bearer token from the dashboard. One token works for any platform.
bots.how2_p_html: Hit POST /v1/ad-request from inside your bot's response handler with the user's query as context.
bots.how3_p_html: Use our helper libs (lumi-discord, lumi-telegram, lumi-slack) to convert the response to native message format.
bots.snippet_h2: A few lines, your existing handler.
bots.snippet_caption_html: Optional helper libraries (lumi-discord, lumi-telegram, lumi-slack) are convenience wrappers around the Lumi API for Bots — they format the ad payload into each platform's native message type. Skip them and roll your own formatting if you'd rather not add a dependency. Full docs →
bots.snippet_shot_caption_html: Same campaign, three native message formats. The helper libs (lumi-discord, lumi-telegram, lumi-slack) handle the platform-specific formatting.
bots.who_h2: If your bot lives in a chat platform, this is your path.
bots.who_li1: You shipped an AI bot on Discord, Telegram, or Slack.
bots.who_li2: Examples: research bots, summarization bots, image generation bots, productivity assistants, customer support bots.
bots.who_li3: You want monetization that doesn't require a paywall — your community uses it free, you earn from ads.
bots.who_li4: You're tired of "tip me on Buy Me a Coffee" being your only option.
bots.rev_row2: Ad impressions per user per day
bots.rev_disclaimer: Numbers are illustrative for a moderately-active community bot. CPMs vary widely by platform — Slack workplace audiences pay more, Telegram consumer audiences less. Vertical and language also matter; English-language productivity bots sit at the top of the range.
bots.faq_h2: The questions every bot dev asks.
bots.faq1_q: Will this violate Discord / Telegram / Slack ToS?
bots.faq1_a: Lumi is designed for ToS compliance. Discord requires clear ad disclosure; we handle that with a "Sponsored" prefix and the disclosure label you configured in your dashboard. Telegram and Slack have looser policies but we follow best practices regardless.
bots.faq2_q: Can I serve image ads, not just text?
bots.faq2_a: Yes. Discord embeds support image fields; Telegram supports inline media; Slack blocks support image accessory components. The helper libraries map a single ad payload to whichever format your platform uses.
bots.faq3_a: Boost Boss serves ads in 40+ languages. CPMs vary by market — large EN, ES, JA markets pay best; smaller-language inventory still fills but at lower rates.
bots.faq4_q: Do I need to handle click tracking myself?
bots.faq4_a_html: No. Use the click_url we return in every ad response — tracking is built into that redirect. Same for impression tracking via impression_url.
bots.faq5_a: 1,000 ad requests per minute per publisher on launch. We raise that for verified-volume publishers; tell us if you're approaching the cap.
bots.cta_p: One REST call. Helper libraries included. Weekly payouts.

nocode.hero_h1_html: Built something with AI but never wrote a server?
nocode.hero_sub: If you built a Custom GPT, a Poe bot, a Perplexity Page, or anything where you didn't ship code — we hear you. The four integration paths Boost Boss ships today are SDK-based and assume you control a server. You probably don't. We're building no-code monetization next, and we'd like you on the waitlist.
nocode.who_h2: If any of these describe what you built, you're in the right place.
nocode.who_sub: These are AI surfaces where the platform owns the runtime — you authored the prompt, the persona, the actions, the knowledge base. You don't deploy a server, and you can't ship an SDK.
nocode.who_card1_p: You built a GPT in OpenAI's GPT Builder. It's listed in the GPT Store or shared privately. People chat with it. You'd like to earn from those conversations.
nocode.who_card2_p: You built a Poe bot — yours or based on a server-side Poe app. Quora's platform handles the runtime. You handle the persona, the knowledge, the prompt design.
nocode.who_card3_p: You write Perplexity Pages with curated AI-generated answers on a topic. You have an audience returning for the content. You'd like a revenue stream.
nocode.who_card4_p: You shipped on Voiceflow, Botpress, Stack AI, FlowiseAI, or another no-code AI platform. You don't write JavaScript. The platform is your runtime.
nocode.honest_h2: Why this is a waitlist, not a "sign up now" button.
nocode.honest_h3: The four shipping paths all need code.
nocode.honest_p1_html: Lumi SDK for MCP, Lumi SDK script tag, Lumi SDK for browser extensions, and Lumi API for Bots all expect a place where you can run logic — a server, a script, an extension manifest. Custom GPT authors and most no-code AI authors don't have that. The platform (OpenAI, Quora, Perplexity, Voiceflow, etc.) owns the runtime.
nocode.honest_p2: We could ship something half-baked here that wraps your prompts in a fragile workaround, but it would break the moment OpenAI updated their Custom GPT runtime, and your users would notice. So we're not doing that.
nocode.honest_p3: Instead we're collecting waitlist signal. If we hear from 30+ no-code authors with real audiences, we ship the integration. If we hear from 3, the path stays parked while we focus on the SDK doors that already work.
nocode.wait_p: One email tells us what you built, where it lives, and roughly how many conversations or interactions it gets per month. If we ship a fit, you're first to know — and the first cohort gets a 90/10 revshare for the first six months, same as our Founding Publishers.
nocode.wait_perk1_l: Get on the list, drop off any time.
nocode.wait_perk2_l: First cohort gets direct input on integration design.
nocode.wait_perk3_l: 90/10 for the first 6 months when we ship.
nocode.fit_yes_li1: You built a Custom GPT and it has actual usage (people are returning to it, or it's listed publicly).
nocode.fit_yes_li2: You shipped a Poe bot and you control the persona / knowledge.
nocode.fit_yes_li3: You write Perplexity Pages with a recurring audience.
nocode.fit_yes_li4: You're on a no-code AI platform (Voiceflow, Botpress, Stack AI) and you'd take ad revenue if it didn't require code.
nocode.fit_yes_li5: You have an idea of how many people interact with your AI per month, even rough.
nocode.fit_no_li1: You wrote your own server or shipped your own SDK — you want one of the four shipping doors instead, not this waitlist.
nocode.fit_no_li2: Your Custom GPT has fewer than ~50 conversations a month — there's no inventory to monetize yet.
nocode.fit_no_li3: The no-code platform you used explicitly bans third-party content injection — we'd hit a policy wall.
nocode.fit_no_li4: You want guaranteed revenue or a per-month minimum — this is unproven inventory; we're not paying advances.
nocode.faq_h2: The questions every no-code author asks first.
nocode.faq1_q: How would the integration even work? Custom GPTs can't load arbitrary code.
nocode.faq1_a: Right — that's the hard part, and the reason this is a waitlist. The most likely shape is a Custom Action that calls a Boost Boss endpoint, returns a sponsored block, and your GPT's instructions tell it to surface the block at the end of relevant answers. It's a constrained version of what the SDK does on a server. We're prototyping; we'll share what works once it does.
nocode.faq2_q: Will OpenAI / Quora / Perplexity allow this?
nocode.faq2_a: This is one of the things we're working out. Each platform has different content policies for what a Custom GPT or bot can serve. Some are clearly within current rules; some are gray. We won't ship a path that gets your GPT delisted, which is part of why we're going slow.
nocode.faq3_a: Honest answer: we don't know yet. The published per-impression CPM for our SDK doors trends $2-15 depending on audience and surface. No-code surfaces are likely to be at the lower end because of disclosure constraints. If your Custom GPT does 10K conversations a month and 1 in 5 surfaces a sponsored block, we're talking double-digit dollars per month at first, more as advertiser demand for AI surfaces matures. This is supplementary income, not a replacement for a job.
nocode.faq4_q: Do you support no-code platforms other than Custom GPTs / Poe / Perplexity?
nocode.faq4_a: If your platform supports calling an HTTP endpoint and rendering returned content (text or markdown), we can probably integrate. Tell us in your email which platform — Voiceflow, Botpress, Stack AI, FlowiseAI, Dust, custom builds — and we'll add it to the prioritization list.
nocode.faq5_q: What if I want to switch from the waitlist to building my own server?
nocode.faq5_a_html: Welcome change of plans. One of our four shipping doors will fit — most likely AI Apps if you're going to deploy a web frontend, or Bots if you're moving to Discord/Telegram/Slack. Same publisher account either way; we move you over.
nocode.faq6_q: Can I just hear from you when this ships, no commitment?
nocode.faq6_a: Yes — just say so in the email. We won't auto-enroll you; the waitlist is informational, not a contract.
nocode.cta_h2: One email. We take it from there.
nocode.cta_p: Tell us what you built, where it lives, and roughly how many people use it.
